Title: Natural and induced variations in transcriptional regulator genes result in low-nicotine phenotypes in tobacco

Regulation_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Burley 21; NC95; Petit Havana SR-I; TI313-derived backcross lines
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Burley 21; NC95; Petit Havana SR-I; TI313-derived backcross lines
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense alkaloid; nicotine is a highly addictive stimulant and toxic environmental contaminant in tobacco products
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates expression of nicotine biosynthetic genes including PMT, QPT2, and A622
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: major positive regulator of nicotine biosynthesis; NIC1 loss has stronger phenotypic impact than NIC2 loss
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ERF199 or reduced ERF199 expression markedly decreases alkaloid accumulation; null nic1 alleles reduce alkaloids to about 25–27% of wild type, while distal deletion alleles reduce them to about 57–73% of wild type
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ERF199 is the key NIC1-locus ERF regulating nicotine biosynthetic genes and alkaloid accumulation in tobacco. 2. Natural deletion allele nic1-3 and induced allele nic1-2, which abolish ERF199 function, severely reduce nicotine-related alkaloids and expression of PMT, QPT2, and A622. 3. Distal deletions in nic1-1 and CRISPR-generated nic1-4 attenuate ERF199 expression without deleting the coding region, causing moderate low-nicotine phenotypes. 4. ERF199 shows functional equivalence to ERF189 in transient transactivation, so its stronger in planta effect likely reflects locus-specific regulation rather than intrinsic activity differences.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genome resequencing; PCR genotyping of deletion alleles; CRISPR/Cas9 generation of nic1-4 and nic1-2-related edited alleles; RT-qPCR of ERF199 and target genes; alkaloid metabolite profiling by GC-MS; transient overexpression/transactivation in Nicotiana benthamiana
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: roots; transcriptome meta-analysis showed slightly higher expression than ERF189 in roots
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: roots; also ectopically overexpressed in leaves for transient assays
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: No reciprocal regulation by ERF189 was detected
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 4-week-old and 7-week-old plant stages; Agrobacterium-mediated transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves for transactivation assays
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S promoter, constitutive (for transient overexpression assays in N. benthamiana)
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: DNA methylation in the 5′ flanking region of ERF199 was examined by bisulfite sequencing in nic1-1 and wild type; no evident increase in methylation in nic1-1, arguing against promoter DNA methylation as the cause of reduced ERF199 expression
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome resequencing: Bioproject PRJNA823906, SRA SRR18653029-SRR18653033; meta-analysis of public transcriptomes: SRR1199063, SRR1199066, SRR1199068-SRR1199074, SRR1199121-SRR1199125, SRR1199127-SRR1199130, SRR1199132, SRR1199135, SRR1199197-SRR1199200, SRR1199202, SRR1199203, SRR955761-SRR955763, SRR955765-SRR955767
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Near-complete shutdown of the nicotine pathway in double ERF knockout backgrounds may cause metabolic imbalance and possible growth penalties or survival limitations
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A major target for breeding low-nicotine tobacco. Natural alleles such as nic1-3 and regulatory alleles such as nic1-1/nic1-4 can be deployed depending on desired nicotine reduction strength; PCR-based genotyping and expression-based screening of ERF199 are recommended
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ERF199 is considered derived from Nicotiana sylvestris ancestry in allotetraploid tobacco. Multiple independent natural deletions around ERF199 suggest it is a mutational hotspot for low-nicotine traits.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense alkaloid; nicotine is a highly addictive stimulant and toxic environmental contaminant in tobacco products
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates expression of nicotine biosynthetic genes including PMT, QPT2, and A622
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: functionally redundant positive regulator of nicotine biosynthesis whose phenotypic contribution becomes clear when ERF199 is compromised
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ERF189 alone has only mild effects on alkaloid accumulation and target gene expression, but strongly enhances low-nicotine phenotypes in combination with nic1/ERF199 mutations
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ERF189 is the NIC2-locus homolog of ERF199 and positively regulates nicotine biosynthetic genes. 2. Natural nic2-1 and edited nic2-2 alleles are likely null alleles but individually cause only limited decreases in alkaloid accumulation. 3. Double mutants with compromised ERF199 function show strongly enhanced reductions in alkaloids and pathway gene expression, revealing functional redundancy. 4. Transient overexpression indicates ERF189 and ERF199 have nearly equivalent transactivation capacity.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genome resequencing and PCR genotyping of nic2-1; CRISPR/Cas9 generation/segregation of nic2-2; RT-qPCR of ERF189 and target genes; alkaloid metabolite profiling by GC-MS; transient overexpression/transactivation in Nicotiana benthamiana
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: roots; accumulates to similar levels as ERF199 in tobacco roots
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: roots; also ectopically overexpressed in leaves for transient assays
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: No reciprocal regulation by ERF199 was detected
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 4-week-old and 7-week-old plant stages; Agrobacterium-mediated transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves for transactivation assays
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S promoter, constitutive (for transient overexpression assays in N. benthamiana)
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome resequencing: Bioproject PRJNA823906, SRA SRR18653029-SRR18653033; meta-analysis of public transcriptomes: SRR1199063, SRR1199066, SRR1199068-SRR1199074, SRR1199121-SRR1199125, SRR1199127-SRR1199130, SRR1199132, SRR1199135, SRR1199197-SRR1199200, SRR1199202, SRR1199203, SRR955761-SRR955763, SRR955765-SRR955767
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Double loss with ERF199 may create metabolic imbalance and possible growth penalties or reduced survival
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful in combination breeding with nic1/ERF199 alleles to achieve stronger nicotine reduction; single nic2 alleles alone are insufficient for major nicotine lowering
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The nic2-1 allele is a large deletion removing ERF189 and neighboring ERFs, but the study concludes that loss of genomic regions other than ERF189 does not significantly contribute to the nicotine phenotype. ERF189 is considered derived from Nicotiana tomentosiformis ancestry in allotetraploid tobacco.
